metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the `make config-check` target (git config --list, configparser, docker compose config -q, bash -n on infra scripts) is in scope as new work to be authored and wired into both test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml, and add it explicitly to the plan's deliverables list so it is not inadvertently scoped out.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'make config-check — parse-validate every infra config … Wired into test.yml (⇒ runs in gerrit-verify ⇒ the Verified gate)' as if this is an existing or straightforwardly addable check, but neither `make config-check` nor any `config-check` target exists in the Makefile, test.yml, or gerrit-verify.yaml. More specifically, the plan's proposed `bash -n on infra/**/*.sh` component of config-check has no prior implementation — the existing CI (test.yml, gerrit-verify.yaml) runs no bash syntax checks on infra scripts at all.
findings.location: Defense-in-depth section: 'make config-check … git config -f {replication,project}.config --list; python configparser on the g2p ini template; docker compose config -q; bash -n on infra/**/*.sh'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `config-check` target to the Makefile that runs: `git config -f infra/gerrit/replication.config --list`, `python -c 'import configparser; configparser.ConfigParser().read("infra/gerrit/gerrit_to_platform.ini.template")'`, `docker compose -f infra/compose/docker-compose.yml config -q`, and `bash -n infra/**/*.sh`. Then add a `make config-check` step to test.yml and gerrit-verify.yaml.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `make config-check` as an explicit step to `.github/workflows/gerrit-verify.yaml`'s `build-and-test` job (mirrors the same step in `test.yml`) so the Verified gate actually enforces the config check on the Gerrit patchset.
findings.finding: `gerrit-verify.yaml` is an unenumerated consumer of the `make config-check` addition to `test.yml`. The gerrit-verify workflow mirrors test.yml's gates inline (not via a composite action), so adding `make config-check` to test.yml does NOT automatically add it to the Gerrit CI gate; the plan's AC ('runs in `gerrit-verify` ⇒ the `Verified` gate') would not hold without a parallel edit to `gerrit-verify.yaml`.
findings.location: Defense-in-depth section / AC: 'CI config-gate: `make config-check` runs in `test.yml`/`gerrit-verify`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step `- name: Config check (make config-check) / run: make config-check` to the `build-and-test` job in `gerrit-verify.yaml`, parallel to how `make lint` and `make typecheck` appear in both files.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `materialize-g2p-config.sh` to the replication path-set's apply action in `autodeploy.sh` (run it after the replication.config file copy), or add `replication.config` to the g2p path-set's change-detect manifest, so g2p's copy is always refreshed when `replication.config` changes.
findings.finding: `materialize-g2p-config.sh` is an unenumerated consumer of `replication.config` changes. The script copies `replication.config` into the g2p config dir so that g2p can discover the GitHub owner/repo. When the autodeploy detects a `replication.config` change and re-materialises it to the Gerrit site dir, the g2p copy in `/var/gerrit/site/g2p-config/replication.config` becomes stale unless `materialize-g2p-config.sh` is re-run. The plan treats `replication` and `g2p` as orthogonal path-sets, but they share this file.
findings.location: What section: per-component change-detect path manifest — 'replication: replication.config; g2p: ini + materialize script'
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, after the replication apply step, unconditionally re-run `materialize-g2p-config.sh` (it is idempotent and non-destructive when the site etc/replication.config is already up-to-date).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md` to the plan scope as an edit target, adding the autodeploy timer install step alongside the existing `install-observability.sh` entry.
findings.finding: `infra/runbooks/provision-restore.md` is an unenumerated consumer. It lists every script to run for a full stack re-provision (compose-up.sh, install-observability.sh, etc.). Adding a new `rebar-autodeploy.timer` systemd unit that must be installed on the box requires adding an `install-autodeploy.sh` (or equivalent) invocation to this runbook, which is not mentioned in the plan scope.
findings.location: Scope section (no mention of runbook updates)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the storm-guard mechanism: to catch a crash-looping *container*, either (a) add a `docker inspect --format '{{.RestartCount}}'` check in the autodeploy loop and emit an alarm when restart count exceeds a threshold, or (b) document that the existing `reviewbot_healthy` CloudWatch metric (observability.sh) already serves as the container-health alarm, and reframe the systemd `StartLimitIntervalSec/StartLimitBurst` accurately as a guard on the *autodeploy job fire rate* (not the container's restart rate).
findings.finding: The plan asserts `systemd StartLimitIntervalSec/StartLimitBurst` on the autodeploy service as an 'outer storm guard' that 'catches a crash-looping container (→ failed, needs reset-failed)'. This is a capability mismatch: `StartLimitIntervalSec/StartLimitBurst` on a systemd oneshot service unit governs how often the *autodeploy job itself* may be started by the timer — it has no visibility into the Docker container managed by `docker compose` with `restart: unless-stopped`. A crash-looping review-bot container is under Docker daemon's restart policy, not systemd's unit-level start-rate limiter. The `reset-failed` hint also applies to the systemd unit, not the container.
findings.location: Stability §4 — 'Outer storm guard' bullet; also AC line 5 ('systemd StartLimit storm-guard is in place')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the 'outer storm guard' claim with: 'Crash-looping container backstop: the existing `reviewbot_healthy` CloudWatch alarm (observability.sh, 5-min period) pages on a down bot; separately, `StartLimitIntervalSec/StartLimitBurst` on `rebar-autodeploy.service` caps runaway timer fires of the deploy job itself (e.g. if the oneshot crashes repeatedly).' This separates the two distinct concerns with accurate mechanisms.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a bootstrap guard for the `:prev` image: on first run (or when `:prev` does not exist), skip the rollback path and document the manual recovery step, OR pre-tag `:prev` during initial environment setup so rollback is always available.
findings.finding: On the very first code-change deploy, no prior `:prev` image exists to tag, so the rollback path (Stability §3: 'rollback to :prev + up -d') has no target — the rollback would fail silently or error, potentially leaving the container in a broken state with no recovery.
findings.location: Stability §3 / What section: 'tag current image :prev, docker compose up -d --build review-bot'
findings.suggested_fix: In autodeploy.sh, before tagging: `if docker image inspect review-bot:prev &>/dev/null || docker tag review-bot:latest review-bot:prev; then ...`. Alternatively, add a step to the existing `compose-up.sh` / initial setup that tags `:prev` immediately after the first successful build.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the on-box pull model decision and its security rationale (no GitHub→AWS OIDC/SSM trust surface) in a living artifact — ADR, CLAUDE.md, or an `infra/docs/autodeploy-design.md` — referenced from the Scope section.
findings.finding: The novel architectural decision — on-box pull model over GitHub-Actions→SSM push, with an explicit security rationale (no OIDC/ssm:SendCommand trust surface) — is captured only in ephemeral plan/PR text, not in a living artifact (ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc). Future maintainers have no durable record of why this design was chosen over the push alternative, which creates churn risk when revisiting CI/CD architecture.
findings.location: ## What — Trigger (RESOLVED) section; ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short ADR (or a section in CLAUDE.md) titled 'Autodeploy: on-box pull vs CI push' covering: the threat model (GH runner as lateral-movement path), the on-box pull mechanism, back-out procedure, and the state-file location (/var/lib/rebar/deployed-sha). Reference it from the Scope line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Nominate and create a `monitoring_autodeploy.tf` (or equivalent named file) in `infra/terraform/` that owns the `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` for `rebar/host:deploy_errors` (and any backoff-state alarm), wired to `aws_sns_topic.alerts`, following the `monitoring_s4b.tf` pattern.
findings.finding: The plan describes a new `deploy_errors` CloudWatch alarm with SNS action (and implies a backoff/failure-state alarm) as part of observability, but does not nominate a Terraform file or resource name for these new alarm resources — leaving them potentially applied out-of-band (not reproducible as-code) rather than tracked in the existing monitoring_*.tf pattern.
findings.location: Observability + back-out section: 'rebar/host:deploy_errors … CloudWatch alarm w/ SNS action'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/terraform/monitoring_autodeploy.tf` with an `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm.autodeploy_errors` resource (namespace=`rebar/host`, metric_name=`deploy_errors`, alarm_actions=[aws_sns_topic.alerts.arn], treat_missing_data=`notBreaching`) and reference it explicitly in the plan's Scope and Observability sections.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup step for Docker image state: after a successful deploy (and after `:prev` is no longer needed as a safety net), run `docker image prune -f` or explicitly remove the superseded `:prev` image; document the retention policy (e.g., keep only current + one rollback generation).
findings.finding: Each autodeploy run builds a new Docker image for the review-bot; dangling image layers accumulate on the host over time because no image pruning or cleanup step is specified. The `:prev` tag is overwritten each cycle, but the previously-untagged layers are never reclaimed.
findings.location: What section / Stability §3: 'docker compose up -d --build review-bot'
findings.suggested_fix: After confirming the new image is healthy and `deployed-sha` is advanced, run: `docker image prune -f --filter 'until=24h'` or `docker rmi $(docker images review-bot --format '{{.ID}}' | tail -n +3)` to keep only the two most recent generations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit wall-clock timeout to the `git fetch` invocation in autodeploy.sh (e.g. `timeout 30s git fetch --depth=1 origin main`) and treat a fetch timeout/failure as a deploy failure that enters the backoff path, so the flock is released promptly and subsequent timer fires proceed.
findings.finding: The `git fetch` step in autodeploy.sh has no stated network timeout, so a hung TCP connection holds the `flock -n` lock and blocks all subsequent 2-minute timer fires for the duration of the hang — a potential gate-freeze window that bypasses the backoff and rollback logic entirely.
findings.location: What / Loop section; Stability §3; autodeploy.sh (implied)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace bare `git fetch origin main` with `timeout 30s git fetch --depth=1 origin main || { log_error 'AUTODEPLOY_ERROR fetch_timeout'; exit 1; }`. The `exit 1` releases the flock, the timer fires the next invocation in 2 min, and the backoff counter increments normally. Also add `--depth=1` to bound transfer size.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact health-check mechanism (e.g., `curl -sf http://localhost:PORT/health` OR a canary-review invocation via the bot's API) so an executor can implement and independently verify AC4 without ambiguity.
findings.finding: The health-check mechanism is described by intent ('bot can actually review, not just process-up') but never specified concretely: no endpoint, no test-review invocation, no CLI command. An executor cannot implement or verify AC4 ('a deliberately-broken bot image FAILS the health check') without deciding what the check actually does.
findings.location: Stability §3; AC4 ('Self-heal: a deliberately-broken bot image FAILS the health check ⇒ rollback to :prev')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a concrete health-check step to autodeploy.sh, e.g.: `docker exec review-bot python -m rebar.health --timeout 25` (or equivalent) that actually exercises the LLM-review path (not just process liveness), and name the same command in AC4 as the proving step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the Terraform file that will own the `deploy_errors` CloudWatch alarm resource in the plan scope (e.g., `infra/terraform/monitoring_autodeploy.tf`), following the established one-file-per-story pattern.
findings.finding: The new Terraform resource for the `deploy_errors` CloudWatch alarm and SNS action is not assigned to any named `.tf` file in the plan scope. The existing pattern (one `monitoring_<story>.tf` per story) implies a new file such as `monitoring_autodeploy.tf`, but this is absent from the scope, leaving the implementer without a clear edit target.
findings.location: Observability + back-out section: 'a CloudWatch alarm w/ SNS action'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/terraform/monitoring_autodeploy.tf` to the scope, following the pattern of `monitoring_s4b.tf` for the `voter_errors` alarm.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly name `infra/scripts/observability.sh` in the plan scope as an edit target, describing the new `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` grep + `rebar/host:deploy_errors` CloudWatch publish section to add (mirroring the voter_errors pattern at lines 95-122).
findings.finding: `infra/scripts/observability.sh` is an unenumerated edit target. The plan says `deploy_errors` metric mirrors `voter_errors` — implying a new `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` grep + CloudWatch publish section in `observability.sh` — but `observability.sh` is not listed in the plan's Scope section and no edit to it is mentioned.
findings.location: Observability + back-out section: '`rebar/host:deploy_errors` (host-probe grep of an `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` marker, mirroring voter_errors)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align terminology in AC3 with the body: either consistently use 'refs/meta/config' (the Gerrit meta-ref) or explicitly state that 'project.config' means the file within refs/meta/config, and clarify what surface (working-tree file vs. meta-ref commits) the detect+signal logic targets.
findings.finding: Terminology drift between body and AC: the body consistently uses 'refs/meta/config' for the deferred/detect-only scope, but AC3 uses 'project.config' — these are distinct concepts (refs/meta/config is a Gerrit meta-ref namespace; project.config is a file within it), which could leave the implementer uncertain about exactly which artifact is being detected-and-signalled vs. auto-applied.
findings.location: AC3 ('project.config changes are detected + signalled, not attempted') vs. 'What' section ('refs/meta/config is OUT of v1') and Scope ('refs/meta/config auto-apply deferred')
findings.suggested_fix: In AC3, replace 'project.config changes' with 'refs/meta/config / project.config changes' and add a parenthetical: '(i.e., commits touching the refs/meta/config meta-ref or its project.config file)' to make the detection target unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define autodeploy.sh thresholds (HEALTH_TIMEOUT_SECS, TIMER_INTERVAL, BACKOFF_BASE_SECS, BACKOFF_CAP_SECS) as named constants at the top of the script (or in a sourced config file), so they are tunable without hunting through logic.
findings.finding: Operational thresholds embedded in autodeploy.sh (30 s end-to-end health-check timeout, 2-min timer cadence, backoff base 60 s / factor 2 / cap 15 m) are stated as fixed values with no mention of making them named constants, environment-variable overrides, or a config block — these are tunable values likely to need adjustment as the bot's startup profile changes.
findings.location: ## Stability — live-pipeline safety §3 (30s timeout); ## What (2-min timer); AC line 5 (backoff params)
findings.suggested_fix: At the top of autodeploy.sh, declare: HEALTH_TIMEOUT_SECS=30; BACKOFF_BASE_SECS=60; BACKOFF_FACTOR=2; BACKOFF_CAP_SECS=900. The systemd timer OnCalendar value can be templated from an override drop-in.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the four-system OSS citation in Stability §4 with a single concrete spec of the backoff (base 60s, factor 2, cap 15m, SHA-keyed reset) without referencing Flux/Argo/k8s systems not present in the stack, to prevent cargo-cult complexity creep in the implementation.
findings.finding: The plan cites four enterprise-scale GitOps/CD systems (Flux retryInterval/backoff, Argo CD retry backoff duration/factor/maxDuration, k8s controller-runtime ItemExponentialFailureRateLimiter 5ms→1000s, systemd v254 RestartSteps/RestartMaxDelaySec) as design precedents for what is ultimately a ~10-line bash capped-exponential-backoff loop in autodeploy.sh. None of these systems are imported, deployed, or available in the stack — they are cited only to justify a well-understood algorithmic pattern. This is a cargo-cult citation pattern: the mechanism is correct and justified by requirements, but the framing over-attributes it to heavyweight systems the stack doesn't use, which can mislead implementers into believing more complexity is intended than is needed.
findings.location: Stability §4 — Failure handling paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: State the backoff spec directly: 'capped exponential backoff: base 60s, factor 2, cap 15m, with a SHA-keyed reset on a new origin/main tip — implemented as a bash counter + sleep in autodeploy.sh.' Remove the four external-system citations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving procedure for AC5: e.g., an integration test script that injects N consecutive failures, asserts inter-attempt sleep times match the backoff formula, then pushes a new SHA and asserts the next attempt fires within one timer cycle (≤2 min + ε).
findings.finding: AC5 states the backoff behavior must be 'proven' and that a new SHA 'resets the backoff (fix-forward deploys promptly)', but no concrete proving command or test procedure is described for verifying exponential retry intervals or SHA-keyed reset behavior in a live or simulated scenario.
findings.location: AC5 ('Failure backoff: repeated failure uses capped exponential backoff (base 60s→cap 15m) ... fix-forward deploys promptly')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the concrete signal mechanism for detected-but-skipped refs/meta/config changes (e.g., emit an `AUTODEPLOY_ERROR` log line with a specific marker, or a dedicated CloudWatch metric), and add a proving command to AC3 that confirms the signal fires when such a change is present on main.
findings.finding: AC3 requires that 'project.config changes are detected + signalled, not attempted', but no proving command or observable for the *signalling* path is described — only detection is implicitly covered by the diff logic.
findings.location: AC3 ('project.config changes are detected + signalled, not attempted'); Stability §2 bullet 'refs/meta/config is OUT of v1 ... a change is DETECTED + signalled, never auto-applied'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the 2-min timer + build-time budget with the ≤5 min AC: either document that the SLA assumes a warm Docker layer cache (and add a cache-warming step to the deploy script), or adjust the SLA to ≤8 min (or split into poll-latency ≤2 min + deploy-completion ≤5 min after detection) so the AC is achievable by construction.
findings.finding: Cross-section tension between the 2-minute timer interval ('What' section) and the ≤5-minute end-to-end latency SLA (AC1): the 2-min poll plus a Docker build (which can take 3-5 min for a Python project with dependency resolution) may sum to >5 min, making the AC unachievable by construction in the general case.
findings.location: 'What' section ('rebar-autodeploy.timer (2-min)') vs. AC1 ('auto-reflects on the box … ≤ 5 min; latency live-measured')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement step (before or during the first live deploy) to record actual P95 bot-container startup time, and confirm or adjust the 30 s health-check timeout from that data rather than leaving it asserted.
findings.finding: The 30 s end-to-end health-check timeout is asserted without a measurement plan for actual bot container startup time post-rebuild. The ACs include 'live end-to-end auto-deploy verified' but do not include a step to measure P95 startup latency and confirm it fits within the timeout. If the container regularly takes longer than 30 s to reach a state where it can 'actually review', the health check will fire spuriously and trigger rollback on every valid deploy.
findings.location: ## Stability §3; AC line 4 (Self-heal AC)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (by reading Gerrit's replication plugin source or testing on the actual box) that `autoReload=true` fires on an in-place bind-mount file copy to `/var/gerrit/site/etc/replication.config`, or document the actual reload mechanism autodeploy.sh will use (e.g., `gerrit plugin reload replication` via admin SSH) that does not require a Gerrit container restart.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'replication → re-materialise (autoReload)' without a Gerrit restart, but setup-replication.sh (lines 13–18, 103–110) explicitly documents that on this instance 'REMOTE PLUGIN ADMIN IS DISABLED' and 'replication.config is loaded at Gerrit startup' with a restart required for the first load. While autoReload=true handles file changes to an already-running plugin, the plan does not verify whether the autodeploy path (a new file copy to /var/gerrit/site/etc/replication.config) will trigger the plugin's file watcher or whether it needs a direct in-process signal. The plan conflates 'copy the file + autoReload picks it up' with the actual mechanism, which setup-replication.sh explicitly says requires a restart when remote plugin admin is disabled.
findings.location: What section — 'Apply only what changed: replication/g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the replication apply strategy with `setup-replication.sh` lines 13-18/103-110: either (a) acknowledge that autoReload covers only in-place edits to an already-loaded config (not first-run), document the residual gap, and signal the operator rather than claiming silent auto-apply; or (b) scope replication.config auto-apply to detect-and-signal only (like `refs/meta/config`), matching the blast-radius rule.
findings.finding: The plan's claim that a `replication.config` change can be applied with 'autoReload / g2p copy' (no Gerrit restart) is directly contradicted by `setup-replication.sh` lines 13-18 and 103-110, which explicitly state: 'Remote plugin admin is DISABLED on this instance, so the new replication.config is loaded at Gerrit STARTUP. RESTART Gerrit to load it.' The `autoReload` key only re-reads a changed file once the plugin is already initialized — it cannot substitute for a Gerrit restart when the plugin needs re-initialization.
findings.location: What section: 'replication: replication.config; ... Apply only what changed: ... replication/g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why established lightweight alternatives (Watchtower for image-watch, `docker compose watch` for source-rebuild, or a thin git-sync+hook agent) were considered and ruled out for this single-box Compose environment, or adopt the simplest applicable tool and scope the custom orchestration only to what it uniquely needs to handle (component-selective apply, replication/g2p config, backoff state).
findings.finding: The plan builds a bespoke shell deploy orchestrator (SHA file, path-diff manifests, rollback, SHA-keyed backoff) without evaluating established lightweight alternatives for a single-box Docker Compose environment — tools like Watchtower (image-level auto-updates), docker compose watch (source-driven rebuilds), or git-sync + post-sync hooks are not mentioned, and the plan cites Flux/Argo CD only to justify backoff parameters, not to explain why a ready-made pull agent was rejected.
findings.location: What section / 'Loop' and 'Apply only what changed' paragraphs; Stability §4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction: either (a) enable remote plugin admin (`ALLOW_REMOTE_RELOAD=1`) so `gerrit plugin reload replication` can hot-apply config changes (verify this works on the live instance), or (b) acknowledge that `replication.config` changes require a Gerrit restart and define the blast-radius-safe restart procedure within the autodeploy safety constraints, or (c) exclude replication.config from auto-apply (detect + signal only, same as refs/meta/config).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that a `replication.config` change can be applied via 're-materialise + autoReload' with no Gerrit restart, but the actual codebase (`setup-replication.sh` lines 13–18 and ADR-0010 § Operations) explicitly states that remote plugin admin is DISABLED on this instance, so replication.config changes require a Gerrit restart to take effect — `autoReload = true` only re-reads the file for subsequent changes once the plugin is already initialized, not on the first (or any config-file-copy-only) apply without a plugin reload.
findings.location: What section / 'Apply only what changed' paragraph: 'replication/g2p → re-materialise (autoReload / g2p copy)'
findings.suggested_fix: The safest approach consistent with the plan's 'never restart Gerrit' constraint: move replication.config to the detect-and-signal category (alongside refs/meta/config), and clarify in the plan that autoReload only eliminates restarts for config changes made *after* the plugin's initial load — not for first-apply or when remote plugin admin is disabled.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm (and enforce in `autodeploy.sh`) that the `origin` remote URL uses `https://` — add an early guard (`git remote get-url origin | grep -E '^https://'` or `git config remote.origin.url` assertion) and document this in the script header; explicitly forbid `git://` on the autodeploy path.
findings.finding: The autodeploy loop's `git fetch origin/main` fetches from a 'public mirror' (GitHub) and immediately uses the result to build+execute a container image, but the plan never confirms the remote URL uses HTTPS (vs. the unauthenticated, unencrypted git:// protocol). A git:// remote on an internet-facing public-subnet EC2 instance is susceptible to network-layer MITM injection of malicious commits into the built image — bypassing the Gerrit two-vote gate, which is a process control rather than a cryptographic wire guarantee.
findings.location: Trigger / Loop section: 'fetch origin/main'; 'the box already reads the PUBLIC mirror read-only'
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to `autodeploy.sh` near the top, after verifying the repo context:
```bash
REMOTE_URL=$(git remote get-url origin)
if [[ "$REMOTE_URL" != https://* ]]; then
  echo "AUTODEPLOY_ERROR: remote 'origin' is not HTTPS ($REMOTE_URL); refusing fetch" >&2
  exit 1
fi
```
This makes the HTTPS constraint explicit, auditable, and enforced at runtime rather than assumed.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
